coredata.dc:title: Effective GQD/AuNPs nanosensors for selectively bifunctional detection of lysine and cysteine under different photophysical properties
coredata.dc:creator: Chaicham, Chiraporn
Tuntulani, Thawatchai
Promarak, Vinich
Tomapatanaget, Boosayarat
coredata.dc:description: This work presented the development of the optical nanosensor based on AuNPs and GQDs which perform the efficiency dual optical sensor. Importantly, the AuNPs with GQDs was prepared by one-pot chemical reaction by using GQDs as an excellent bifunction of the reducing agent and stabilizer to obtain the disaggregated AuNPs/GQDs in red color property. Apart from its photophysical sensing ability, this hybrid material demonstrated the dual sensing of cysteine and lysine regarding to colorimetric and fluorometric techniques, respectively, in PBS solution pH 7.4. The presence of Cys in the AuNPs/GQDs solution rapidly lead to the color change from red to blue with concomitant of the red shift of the absorption band at 525 to 645 nm undergone the aggregation process. On the other hand, AuNPs/GQDs nanosensor in the presence of Lys performs the off-on fluorescence at 450 nm without color change in PBS solution pH 7.4. Interestingly, no interference of any amino acids towards Cys and Lys detection highlighted a high efficacy in selective sensing aspect. For sensing application, the detection limit of Cys and Lys was 5.88 μM and 16.14 μM, respectively, with an acceptable % recovery of 101–106 in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Gold nanoparticles
Aggregation
Lysine
Cysteine
Displacement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots/gold nanoparticles (AuNPs/GQDs) hybrid materials
Determination of target molecules in biological samples
Preparation and characterization of AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite
Photophysical studies of nanomaterials and various amino acids
Study on the interaction mechanism of GQDs/AuNPs nanocomposite
Interference studies of GQDs/AuNPs nanomaterial towards amino acids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of formation of AuNPs/GQDs particles and inset photographical images with various concentrations of NaAuCl4 in the range of 0.03 – 0.33 mM.
FT-IR spectra of GQDs (black line) and AuNPs/GQDs (red line) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Comparison properties of the TEM images and EDX spectra of as-prepared of GQDs (a) and AuNPs/GQDs (b).
(A) XPS full scan spectra of GQDs and AuNPs/GQDs (B, C) the XPS analysis of C1 s and O1 s of GQDs (D, E and F) the XPS analysis of C1 s, O1 s and Au4f of AuNPs/GQDs, respectively.
The determination of stability of AuNPs/GQDs hybrid materials before and after removing the inorganic residue by monitoring the absorption band at 525 nm.
The absorption (a) and emission (b) spectra of 0.4 μM AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite with various amino acids and the visual studies.

Determination of zeta potential of nanocomposite of GQDs (black), AuNPs/GQDs (red), AuNPs/GQDs ⊂Cys (blue) and AuNPs/GQDs ⊂Lys (green) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
TEM images and particle sizes of AuNPs/GQDs (a), AuNPs/GQDs⊂Cys (b), AuNPs/ GQDs⊂Lys (c) and inserted graphical photograph.
The proposed mechanism of AuNPs/GQDs hybrid materials with Cys and Lys.
Interference studies of AuNPs/GQDs (0.4 μM) toward Cys (a) and Lys (b) with 50 equiv other amino acids in 1 mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4).
The visual changes of AuNPs/GQDs (1.68 × 10−5 M) nanomaterials with varying amount of Cys (a) and Lys (b).
The analytical comparison for determination of l-cysteine and l-lysine between this work and other works.

row.entry: Cu complex of quinoline sulfonic acid (CuQ2)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of cysteine in urine samples and Lys in Milk samples.

head.ce:title: Effective GQD/AuNPs nanosensors for selectively bifunctional detection of lysine and cysteine under different photophysical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Supramolecular Chemistry Research Unit, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Phayathai Road, Patumwan, Bangkok, 10330, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Molecular Science and Engineering, Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (VISTEC), Wangchan, Rayong, 21210, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Molecular Science and Engineering
Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (VISTEC)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presented the development of the optical nanosensor based on AuNPs and GQDs which perform the efficiency dual optical sensor. Importantly, the AuNPs with GQDs was prepared by one-pot chemical reaction by using GQDs as an excellent bifunction of the reducing agent and stabilizer to obtain the disaggregated AuNPs/GQDs in red color property. Apart from its photophysical sensing ability, this hybrid material demonstrated the dual sensing of cysteine and lysine regarding to colorimetric and fluorometric techniques, respectively, in PBS solution pH 7.4. The presence of Cys in the AuNPs/GQDs solution rapidly lead to the color change from red to blue with concomitant of the red shift of the absorption band at 525 to 645 nm undergone the aggregation process. On the other hand, AuNPs/GQDs nanosensor in the presence of Lys performs the off-on fluorescence at 450 nm without color change in PBS solution pH 7.4. Interestingly, no interference of any amino acids towards Cys and Lys detection highlighted a high efficacy in selective sensing aspect. For sensing application, the detection limit of Cys and Lys was 5.88 μM and 16.14 μM, respectively, with an acceptable % recovery of 101–106 in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids are essential molecules for biological systems for example, it plays a pivotal of building block in protein synthesis [1], crucial role in maintaining cellular redox environments and mitigating damage from free radicals and toxins, moreover, the balancing nervous system to maintain in human body [2]. Importantly, two amino acids (Cys and Lys) are necessarily needed to infants and human health. Cysteine (Cys) is a semi-essential α-amino acid which can be biosynthesized in humans [3]. Cys acts as a reagent in many catalytic reactions in biological system and plays a pivotal role in inducible, endogenous detoxification mechanisms in organs [4,5]. The abnormal level of Cys is responsible for many diseases involving liver damage, skin lesions, slow growth in the children and lethargy [6]. l-lysine is a significant essential amino acid for human body. It exhibits the key role in the carnitine product that is a nutrient for converting the fatty acids into energy and helping lower cholesterol, a substance important for bones and connective tissues including skin, tendons, and cartilage [7,8]. The deficiency of Lys is involved in fatigue, loss of appetite, slow growth, anemia and reproductive disorders [9]. Therefore, those amino acids are important parameter for various kinds of difference diagnosis diseases [10,11]. The analytical methods to detect Cys and Lys have been reported, especially in chemiluminescence [12–14], chromatography [15] and electrochemistry [16–18]. All of techniques that mentioned above, they have the high sensitivity but also had many disadvantages of high costs and time-consuming steps. Nowadays, the nanomolecular sensors have been developed to detect biomedical molecules due to chemical stability, biocompatibility and easy surface modification [19,20].

The biosensing applications of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been reported in a range of biological environment. The sulfhydryl (thiol) and amine side chain of amino acids could covalently bound with AuNPs to induce an aggregation of AuNPs resulting in color change from red to blue upon concentration of amino thiol molecules [21,22]. Gold nanoparticles could be apply to measure the concentration of amino acids, however, the small biothiol molecules such as glutathione (GSH) and homocysteine (Hcy) can interfere the amino acid detection due to composed of thiol moiety in molecule [6,10,23,24].

Use of luminescent quantum dots (QDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) in energy transfer applications have generated a great deal of attention and much activity in the past decades [25,26]. Jongjinakool and co-workers [27] demonstrated the simple, sensitive and selective colorimetric method for the detection of cysteine by unmodified gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as probes which exhibited the dramatically color changes from red-purple-blue due to the occurrence of nanoparticle aggregation induced by Cys. Tang and co-workers [28] reported the fluorescent nanoprobe synthesized by amino N-dots combine with AuNPs (aNdots/AuNPs) which performed the turn off-on fluorescence by Cys because of thiol species of Cys bound on AuNPs surface inducing disaggregation of AuNPs. Moreover, Deng and co-workers [13] developed the multi-signals of nanosensor composite of AuNPs supported on nitrogen-doped C-dots (NC-dots) for discrimination of biothiol compounds using electrochemical carbonization method. Upon adding Cys incubated with NaOH (pH 12) for 12 h, the absorbance band of NC-dots/AuNPs hybrids shifted from 680 nm to 525 nm with a respect to the change of the color solution of nanohybrids from purple to red and the recovered fluorescence of NC-dots. From above researches, there are recent advances in engineering a variety of nanoparticles with several unique optical and physical properties, however, no reports demonstrated the multi-functioned sensor by nanohybrid of AuNPs/graphene quantum dots.

Herein, we have demonstrated a one-pot chemical reduction of AuCl4 − by using GQDs as bifunctions of reductant and stabilizer which were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method at 160 °C. The obtained AuNPs with GQDs exhibited the red color of AuNPs and non-fluorescent GQDs on AuNPs. The GQDs excellently facilitate the formation and stabilization of AuNPs, resulting in core-shell structure of AuNPs/GQDs. Of particular sensing ability of AuNPs/GQDs hybrid materials has been studied and it was found that this nanocomposite performed an efficiency of dual optical detection. The presence of cysteine in the solution of AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite in PBS pH7.4 induced the blue color solution due to the self-aggregation of AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite. On the other hand, this nanocomposite exhibited a recovery fluorescence and the remained red solution upon the addition of Lys under the displacement of Lys on the GQDs. Notably, the presence of high concentration of other amino acids and biothiol compounds does not interfere the sensing approach of AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite towards Cys and Lys. This highlights the easy to prepare AuNPs/GQDs nanosensor with a facile sensing operation, easy to use and friendly environment and especially very high selectivity for Cys and Lys sensing.

Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Merck. Sodium tetrachloroaurate (III) dehydrate (NaAuCl4.2H2O 99%) and all amino acids (Cys, Lys, Hcy, Gly, Thr, Phe, Ala, Leu and Met) were brought from Sigma Aldrich. l-glutathione (GSH, reduced > 98%) was received from Calbiochem. Dialysis sacks in MWCO 2000 Da was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Milli-Q ultrapure water was used for experimental analysis.

Absorption spectra were measured on a Varian Cary 50 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were performed on a Varian spectrofluorometer by personal computer data processing unit. The light source is Cary Eclipse a pulsed xenon lamp and a detector is a photomultiplier tube. The nanohybrid materials was characterized by High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) on an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEOL JEM 2010 with a field emission gun operated at 200 kV. The TEM micrographs were used to determine size of the deposited particles by counting approximately 200 particles (ImageJ 1.49 V free software with Java 1.6.0 65(32-bit) obtained from https://imagej.nih.gov/ij/, Wayne Rasband, National Institutes of Health, USA). The particle sizes and microscopic charge states of nanosensors were measured by using Nano Zetasizer 90, Malvern instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots/gold nanoparticles (AuNPs/GQDs) hybrid materials
ce:section.ce:para: To perform AuNPs/GQDs hybrid nanomaterials, the 40 μL of 0.01 M aqueous solution of NaAuCl4.2H2O was rapidly added into the 200 μL of GQDs solution (64.06 mg/mL). Then the solution was adjusted to 3 mL milli-Q water and vigorously stirred at room temperature. After 5 min, the color solution turned from yellow to red and then the solution was continuously stirred for 20 min to ensure the complete reaction. A ruby red color solution was observed. The mixture solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove the inorganic residues. The precipitate products were redispersed in 3 mL of 1 mM phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) to obtain AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite for further assay.

Typically, a stock solution (1 mM) of Cys and Lys were prepared in 1 mM PBS buffer solutions (pH 7.4). For the determination of Cys, 200 μL of AuNPs/GQDs solutions (64.06 mg/mL) were dissolved in PBS. The different concentration of Cys in the range of 0 - 0.5 mM was added and diluted to 2 mL in PBS solution under stirring for 2 min. The absorption spectra were recorded. In the case of Lys detection, AuNPs/GQDs solutions were prepared by following as the same method but the use of Lys in the range of 0 – 0.8 mM. The determination of Lys was examined by using fluorescence technique. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of target molecules in biological samples

ce:list-item.ce:para: Preparation of Cys in urine sample

ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, the synthetic urine was prepared [29–31] as the following in Table S1 in supporting information. Urine sample was collected from three people in our laboratory and kept at −20 °C for 24 h for the protein residue precipitate. The sample was melted at room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate the protein residues. The supernatant was kept measuring the amount of Cys. The standard addition method was used to explore the efficacy of target molecule. The 200 μL urine sample was separately added into 200 μL AuNPs/GQDs and the solution was diluted to 2 mL. The mixture solution was stirred at ambient temperature for 2 min prior to measurement. The absorption spectra were recorded in the range of 200–800 nm.
-
Preparation of Lys in milk sample

The Lys in milk samples including milk and soybean milk was examined. The 5 g of sample was dissolved in 20 mL milli-Q water. The sample solution was stirred until homogeneous solution. They were separated by centrifuge under 10,000 rpm for 10 min to obtain a clear solution. The different concentrations of Lys (0 – 0.5 mM) were added into 200 μL milk sample and 200 μL AuNPs/GQDs. The mixture solution was adjusted to 2 mL and was stirred for 2 min prior to measurement of the fluorescence spectra under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The colloidal gold nanoparticles were prepared by redox reaction method using GQDs as the reducing agent and stabilizer. The NaAuCl4 was reduced by GQDs to produce AuNPs lead to form AuNPs/GQDs hybrid nanomaterials. To verify the proper ratio of GQDs and NaAuCl4, the UV–vis spectroscopy was carried out with various concentration of Au3+ in the range of 0.03 – 0.33 mM as shown in Fig. 1 . The absorption spectra of AuNPs at 525 nm were increased upon increasing the concentration of NaAuCl4 and the maximum absorbance was observed upon the addition of AuNPs at 0.20 mM. However, in the higher concentration of AuNPs over 0.13 mM, the shoulder of this absorption band (around 600–750 nm) was remarkedly developed. This result was stemmed from an aggregation of AuNPs of which corresponds to the visually color change from red to purple. These results suggested that the 0.13 mM of NaAuCl4 in the presence of GQDs (64.06 mg/mL) is a suitable concentration to form the colloidal hybrid sensor of AuNPs/GQDs which provided the intense red color solution with the high absorbance at 525 nm without the aggregation AuNPs at 645 nm. Of particular fluorescence response of GQDs/AuNPs was also investigated in Fig. S4 showing a large fluorescence quenching at 475 nm regarding to the energy transfer between AuNPs and GQDs. It indicated that GQDs performed a benefit of reducing agent and stabilizer which prevented an aggregation for AuNPs based on negative repulsion of carboxylate moiety of GQDs. Consequently, this concentration of NaAuCl4 was suitably used to form GQDs/AuNPs nanocomposite.

The as-synthesized AuNPs/GQDs was characterized by Fourier-transform (FT-IR) spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 2 . The vibrational absorption of hydroxyl group (OH) of as-prepared GQDs showed a board peak at 3250 cm−1. The stretching vibrational C–H of GQDs appeared at 2966 cm−1 and 1281 cm−1, implying some incompletely carbonized citric acid. Additionally, two evident absorption peaks of COO− were attributed at 1556 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1, corresponding to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively. The typical mode of phenolic of COH stretching was attributed at 1078 cm−1 Comparing the FT-IR spectra of GQDs and AuNPs/GQDs, the relative intensity of COO- vibrational absorption slightly increased, causing that the GQDs was partially oxidized by NaAuCl4. Due to the hydroxyl carboxylic-containing GQDs, the as-prepared AuNPs/GQDs exhibited a good dispersion in the aqueous solution.

To further confirm the structural information of GQDs/AuNPs nanomaterials, the TEM images and EDX spectra have been investigated. From TEM images of GQDs presented the homogeneous dispersion with an average size about 5 nm calculated from 150 particles by using the ImageJ software. After adding NaAuCl4, the TEM image of Fig. 4b exhibited the intense dark particles assigned to AuNPs which was encapsulated by the light shell of GQDs nanosheets. Moreover, we found the significant increase of an average particle size for the GQDs/AuNPs nanomaterials at ∼15 nm A larger average size of as-synthesized AuNPs/GQDs is due to the possible aggregation of GQDs on the surface of gold nanoparticles. Besides the different size, the morphologies of TEM images of GQDs (in Fig. 3 a) and GQDs/AuNPs (in Fig. 3b) was relatively different.

The EDX analysis was used to evaluate the element composition of AuNPs/GQDs. They all contained mainly C and O elements causing the pyrolysis of citric acid. In the case of AuNPs/GQDs nanomaterials, the additional peak of Au element was observed possibly caused by gold-containing particles. These results confirmed that the component of Au was hybrid on the surface of GQDs as shown in the Fig.3b. The compositions of elements of nanohybrid material were showed in Fig. 3. These results reasonably revealed that the preparation of the AuNPs/GQDs nanomaterials has been achieved.

Additionally, to gain more information about composition of the prepared GQDs and AuNPs/GQDs, the chemical compositions of both materials were also investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as shown in Fig. 4 . The XPS full scan spectrum of GQDs presented two main peaks of C 1 s, and O 1 s at 283.75 and 528.33 eV, respectively, while that of AuNPs/GQDs presented two main peaks of C 1 s, at 284.10 eV and O 1 s at 529.58 eV and minor peak of Au 4f at 83.40 eV. It is indicative of the chemical composite for GQDs and AuNPs/GQDs consisting of a dominant C, and O elements and the additional minor peak of Au for AuNPs/GQDs in their structures. Regarding to spectrum of each element, the C 1 s scan spectrum exhibited the characteristic of four peaks including CC and CC at 283.89 eV, COH at 285.32 eV, C-O-C at 286.88 eV and CO at 288.67 eV indicating the abundant carbon structure [32–34]. Moreover, the peak of O 1 s showed the binding energy that can be fitted into four peaks at 528.33, 530.68, 531.74 and 533.83 eV which correspond to COH, CO, COC and CO, respectively.

In the case of AuNPs/GQDs, the XPS spectrum of C 1s scan spectrum displayed four peaks at 284.20, 285.46, 287.23 and 288.84 eV, corresponding to CC or CC, COH, COC and CO, respectively [35]. In the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum, four fitted peaks of binding energy at 529.58, 530.84, 532.27, and 533.94 eV could be assigned to COH, CO, COC, and CO, respectively. Importantly, the observation of Au4f in XPS spectrum of AuNPs/GQDs provided the strong evidence to confirm the obtained AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite. Of particular Au 4f peak demonstrated two peaks of Au 4f 7/2 and Au 4f 5/2 at 83.40 and 87.03, respectively [36,37]. Deeply comparison of XPS spectrum of GQDs, the XPS spectrum of AuNPs/GQDs exhibited a slightly higher binding energy possibly caused by an asymmetric structure from Au adsorbed on GQDs [38].

Taking on board of the benefit of nanocomposite in term of stability that we have particularly compared in stability of nanomaterials before and after centrifuge to remove the inorganic residue by using UV–vis spectroscopy. The stability of AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite was measured by following the absorption peak at 525 nm. In the unpurified AuNPs/GQDs, a large absorption change was observed within 2 days. In comparison, the stability of AuNPs/GQDs after removing the inorganic residue remained constant for at least 1 month as shown in Fig. 5 . The stability of GQDs/AuNPs was improved by centrifugation to remove the inorganic residue and the result showed an aggregation of a purified AuNPs/GQDs was retarded over a month. These results indicated that GQDs served as a good stabilizer of gold nanoparticles to excellently prevent an aggregation of AuNPs nanoparticles based on high negative repulsion of carboxylate-containing group of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical studies of nanomaterials and various amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical properties of nanomaterials was screening test with various amino acids in both techniques of UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy.

As shown in Fig. S3 spectrum, AuNPs/GQDs showed the broad absorption band with typical absorption peak centered at 525 nm, regarding to their surface plasmon resonance [30]. For UV–vis properties of GQDs/AuNPs and various amino acids in PBS (pH 7.4) shown in Fig. 6 , In the presence of Cys, the maximum absorption peak of AuNPs/GQDs at 525 nm was decreased with a concomitant of a new absorption band around 645 nm. Upon the addition of other amino acid, the absorption spectra of AuNPs/GQDs were insignificant changed. Considering the visually responsive solution, the color solution of AuNPs/ GQDs rapidly turned from red to blue in the case of only Cys. Particularly, the well-defined properties of gold nanoparticles are that the typical aggregation of gold nanoparticle performs blue color of AuNPs solution and red-shifted absorption band. These phenomenon of AuNPs/GQDs with Cys suggested the aggregation of AuNPs/GQDs undergone the sulfhydryl side chain. On the other hand, AuNPs/GQDs did not show any obvious change in the UV–vis spectra and colour solution after adding glutathione and homocysteine which have a similar structure consisting of thiol, carboxylic and amine groups. This can be ascribed that the strong steric hindrance effect of a large structure [33] of glutathione which prevents the bonding interaction with AuNPs. In comparison, the size of Cys is a smaller structure which preferentially interacted with AuNPs/GQDs through Au-S bond and zwitterion of COO− and NH3 + with AuNPs leading to aggregation of AuNPs. In the case of homocysteine, it can form a stable five-membered ring transition structure with a pretreatment at neutral or basic condition.

Apart from the benefit fluorescence property of GQDs, the AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposites have been investigated in fluorescence response towards various amino acids in 1 mM PBS. As seen in Fig. 6b, the strongest emission band at approximately 450 nm was obviously observed in the presence of Lys while other amino acid showed the similar emission band of the AuNPs/GQDs nanomaterials. According to the fluorescence properties of sensors of GQDs, the strongest emission band at approximately 450 nm was obviously observed in the presence of Lys while other amino acid showed the similar emission band of the AuNPs/GQDs nanomaterials. According to the fluorescence properties of GQDs, the subsequent photoluminescence recovery of GQDs in AuNPs/GQDs was appeared and the red color solution remained unchanged (inset of Fig. 6b). Collectively, these results demonstrated that GQDs was probably desorbed on the AuNPs possibly caused by the competition between amino acid group of Lysine and oxygen-containing functional group of GQDs. Consequently, Lys performed the stabilizer of AuNPs instead of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the interaction mechanism of GQDs/AuNPs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the hypotheses, the zeta-potential (ζ-potential) analysis was used to examine the magnitude of electrostatic charge on surface of particles. The microscopic charge state of GQDs/AuNPs particles was about −52 mV ascribing the excellent disaggregation of nanoparticles resulting in the red color solution of AuNPs/GQDs. This means the good stabilizer of GQDs on AuNPs. While low colloidal stability of AuNPs in the presence of Cys, it was found that Cys induced the slightly shift to the positive charge at −44 mV resulting in the blue color solution with a respect of the aggregation of particles. In the case of Lys, the total charge state exhibited at −32 mV which is more positive charge than free AuNPs/GQDs and the mixture solution of AuNPs/GQDs and Cys (Fig. 7 ). However, the color solution of AuNPs/GQDs in the presence of Lys displayed a red solution with an unchanged absorption band at 525 nm and turn-on fluorescence at 450 nm. These results implied the disaggregation of particles. As assumption, it can be explained that the desorption process of GQDs on the surface of AuNPs/GQDs nanocomposite was occurred and the Lys was assembly bound with the surface of AuNPs and performed as the good stabilizer. Due to the repulsion of the ammonium-containing functional group of Lys, its benefit takes the reductant aggregation of AuNPs even in the low total negative charge on the surface of AuNPs.

Furthermore, the TEM images were used to investigate the sensing mechanism of GQDs/AuNPs. The morphology of unique colloidal nanosensor displayed an average particles size of 9 nm regarding to a spherical AuNPs which was supported on GQDs surface (Fig. 8 a). Upon the addition of Cys into the solution of nanosensors, the self-aggregation of AuNPs/GQDs ⊂Cys showed a significant increase of diameter size of AuNPs to be 400 nm as shown in Fig. 8b. In the high magnification of Fig. 8c inset, the graphene nanosheets were remarkably removed off AuNPs. These TEM images suggested a strong evidence to support that Cys enabled to induce the self-aggregation of AuNPs/GQDs while Lys preferred to perform the stabilizer by displacement of GQDs sheets resulting in turn-on fluorescence at 450 nm corresponding to GQDs. For a well-ascribed sensing approach, the cartoon of proposed mechanism of AuNPs/GQDs towards Cys and Lys was reliably illustrated in Fig. 9 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference studies of GQDs/AuNPs nanomaterial towards amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The interference studies of nanohybrid of AuNPs/GQDs towards Cys and Lys with 50 equiv other amino acids were examined by using UV–vis and fluorometric methods, respectively. In the case of Cys, the ratio of the absorption band of GQDs/AuNPs at 525 nm and that in the presence of Cys at 645 nm in 1 mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4) was monitored to evaluate the effect of interference toward Cys as shown in Fig. 10 a. It was found that the relative A525/A645 of the nanosensor in the presence of Cys (GQDs/AuNPs⊂Cys) was approximately 1.5 while the those of GQDs/AuNPs⊂Cys upon the addition of different amino acids exhibited the analog results. The constant absorbance ratio is a strong support that the other amino acid could not interfere the quantitative analysis of Cys. This is indicative of a highly selective detection of Cys without the effect of interference. On the other hand, the interference studies of other amino acids towards Lys was also examined in 1 mM PBS pH 7.4 by using fluorescence technique (Fig. 10b). The fluorescent intensity of GQDs/AuNPs⊂Lys is similar to that of GQDs/AuNPs⊂Lys in the presence of 50 eqv other amino acids. This satisfied the high selectivity for Lys detection without interference from other amino acids. As the advantage of dual detection of AuNPs/GQDs toward Lys and Cys, the interference was presumably occurred in each other for detection even in difference techniques. Fascinatingly, the results from UV–vis and Fluorescent methods reliably promoted the high-specific detection Cys and Lys without interruption of each other.

According to the specific-binding nanosensor, the quantitative analysis with Cys and Lys was carried out by using UV–vis and Fluorescence techniques in 1 mM PBS (pH 7), respectively. To evaluate the sensitivity of our nanosensor toward Cys, the UV–vis titration of AuNPs/GQDs developing at 645 nm (Fig. S5a) was measured and the limit of detection provided 5.88 μM in the linear range of 0.005-0.100 mM with R2 = 0.98. For Lys sensing studies in Fig.S5b, the fluorescence titration of AuNPs/GQDs nanomaterials was gradually increased upon the increment of Lys. The limit of detection of this materials towards Lys showed at 16.14 μM in the linear range of 0.05-0.5 mM with R2 = 0.99. In comparison with some recently reported of Cys detection, the nanosensor based on AuNPs presented a high sensitivity and low detection limit as shown in Table 1 [13,28,39,40]. Normally, the sulfhydryl moiety responded mainly to AuNPs as following by colorimetric and fluorescent detection. Consequently, the strong interference of biothiol molecules is seriously problem. Moreover, the Lys sensing system based on the colorimetric assay of nanosensor associated with AuNPs was also listed in Table 1. Our work showed a slightly higher detection limit compared to other works but the benefit of our nanosensor demonstrated a bifunctional sensing approach with a high selectivity towards Lys and Cys without interference as well as rapid detection time.

Extensive work for visual determination of this nanosensor toward Cys and Lys was carried out under visible and UV lamp at 365 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the visually quantitative behavior of this nanomaterials towards the specific guests have been beneficially determined for easy analysis. Fig. 11 a showed a visualized-color change of AuNPs/GQDs regarding to an increment of concentration of Cys and Lys. The result exhibited a visual detection limit with Cys was approximately 0.012 mM (1.44 ppm) with the color change from red to blue. In the case of Lys detection, the tendency of blue brightness of the AuNPs/GQDs solution with Lys under UV lamp has been raised in regarding to an increment of Lys concentration with the visual detection limit of 70 μM (10.25 ppm) (Fig. 11b). Noticeably, GQDs/AuNPs offered the powerful visual detection of Cys and Lys in ppm level under the UV light and UV lamp at 365 nm, respectively.

To verify the accuracy and reliability of the dual detection for AuNPs/GQDs, the real samples of urine and milk were utilized for measurement of Cys and Lys, respectively. As shown in Table 2 , % recovery of Cys and Lys measured by AuNPs/GQDs is in the range from 101.60 to 105.83 and 103.49–105.47, respectively, and RSD is very low values indicating a good accuracy and repeatability of the develop method in real sample analysis (the calibration curves was shown in Fig S6-S8.) The results suggested that our strategy has potential for highly selective and quantification of Cys and Lys in real samples.

We have been successfully designed and engineered the nanohybrids to detect amino acid in PBS buffer pH 7.4. The sensor GQDs/AuNPs have been obtained under one-pot synthesis by using GQDs as a reductant and stabilizer of the AuNPs. The GQDs/AuNPs were prepared via in situ growth of AuNPs on the surface of GQDs. This nanohybrid material is extremely stable in aqueous medium with an intensive surface plasma band at 525 nm. The GQDs/AuNPs was found to be dual detection of Cys and Lys based on the UV–vis and fluorescence techniques, respectively. In the case of Cys, the self-aggregation of GQDs/AuNPs has been demonstrated by the observation of color change from red to blue and the red shift of absorption band from 525 nm to 645 nm. Moreover, this material was found to determine Lys by “turn-on” fluorescent at 450 nm with a respect to emission band of free GQDs. This manner underwent the displacement process between GQDs sheets and Lys on AuNPs surface. The nanocomposite of GQDs/AuNPs can be used to measure the concentration of Cys and Lys without interference in the detection limit of 5.88 μM and 16.14 μM under linear range of 0.05-0.5 mM and 0.047-0.8 mM, respectively. Furthermore, GQDs/AuNPs highlighted a promising quantitative determination of Cys in urine samples and Lys in milk samples with real time analysis and the acceptable %recovery in the range of 101.68–105.83%. On the benefit of analytical application in biological system, the use of AuNPs/GQDs nanomaterials as the chemosensor highlighted a good quantitative determination of Cys in urine samples and Lys in milk samples with real time analysis and highly specific detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Thailand Research Fund (RSA6080012 and RTA6080005) and the 90th Anniversary of Chulalongkorn Fund (Ratchadapisek Sompoch Endowment Fund) for research grant. CC is the pH.D student supported by the Human Resource Development in Science Project (Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand, SAST).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.150.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chiraporn Chaicham was received her Ph.D. (2017) degrees in Chulalongkorn University under supervision of Professor Boosayarat Tomapatanaget. She received the Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand for her study. Her research interest is host-guest chemistry including synthesis of a new sensory molecule for detection of biothiol, nanotechnology for sensing application by using the fluorescence, UV–vis techniques.

Thawatchai Tuntulani is currently a professor in the Department of Chemistry at Chulalongkorn University, Thailand. He earned his PhD in Chemistry with Prof. Marcetta Y. Darensbourg at Texas A&M University, USA after obtaining a Bachelor Degree of Engineering from Chiang Mai University, Thailand. He did his postdoctoral studies with Prof. Jean-Marie Lehn before joining Chulalongkorn University in 1995. His research interests include syntheses of derivatives of calix[4]arene and other macrocyclic compounds and development of electrochemical and optical sensing materials for ions and biomolecules.

Vinich Promarak is currently a professor in the Institute of Molecular Science and Engineering at Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (VISTEC), Thailand. He earned his pH.D. in chemistry with Prof. Paul L. Burn at Oxford University, UK in 2002. His research interests include the development of new functional organic materials for applications in optoelectronic devices and sensors.

Boosayarat Tomapatanaget is currently an assistant professor Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University. She received her B.S. (1995) and Ph.D. (2003) degrees in Chulalongkorn University under supervision of Professor Thawatchai Tuntulani. She received the Royal Golden Jubilee Scholarship from the Thailand Research Fund to do research under supervision of Professor. Paul D. Beer at the University of Oxford, UK. Her research interest is host-guest chemistry including synthesis of a new sensory molecule, nanotechnology for sensing application by using the fluorescence, UV-visible and electrochemical techniques for detection of anions, cations and neutral molecules.
